title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Mr. Kitcher, an investigator for Scotland Yard, questions Margaret regarding the history of the Ives couple. Margaret expresses fear and insists that Kitcher promise not to seek out Ambrose Flavard or mention Patrick and Francis Ives to anyone in the village. She explains that a tragedy in 1913 left "invisible wounds" on the residents of Glebeholme, including Nancy Duane and Richard Nigg, and that Ambrose is suffering as well. Reluctantly, Kitcher swears to keep her words secret, and Margaret begins to recount the history of the Ives family.

Sixteen years prior, a maid working for Reverend Patrick Ives spread a malicious rumor that Patrick was a fraud who used spiritualism to claim he could communicate with the deceased in order to swindle villagers out of money. The maid told only one person: Harriet Sipper, a bitter woman who enjoyed exposing the faults of others. Harriet escalated the lie, claiming Patrick had already defrauded several people. 

The rumor specifically targeted Nancy Duane, who had moved to the village in 1910 after marrying the schoolmaster William Duane. In January 1913, Harriet spread the claim that Nancy made secretive late-night visits to the vicar's house while his wife, Francis, was away. Despite the difficulty of verifying such claims in the dark, the villagers of Grellhollow condemned Patrick. This condemnation was supported by Ada Granceberry, a devout woman, and Richard Nix, a respected lawyer. Nix claimed to have personally witnessed a woman resembling Nancy entering and leaving the vicar's house during those times.
